scene_title: A tent in the French camp

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: O thou good Kent, how shall I live and work,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: To match thy goodness? My life will be too short,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: And every measure fail me.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To be acknowledged, madam, is o’erpaid.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: All my reports go with the modest truth;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Nor more nor clipp’d, but so.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Be better suited:

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: These weeds are memories of those worser hours:

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: I prithee, put them off.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Pardon me, dear madam;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Yet to be known shortens my made intent:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: My boon I make it, that you know me not

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Till time and I think meet.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Then be’t so, my good lord.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: To the Doctor. How does the king?

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Madam, sleeps still.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: O you kind gods,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Cure this great breach in his abused nature!

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: The untuned and jarring senses, O, wind up

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Of this child-changed father!

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: So please your majesty

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: That we may wake the king: he hath slept long.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Be govern’d by your knowledge, and proceed

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: I’ the sway of your own will. Is he array’d?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Ay, madam; in the heaviness of his sleep

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: We put fresh garments on him.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Be by, good madam, when we do awake him;

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: I doubt not of his temperance.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Very well.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Please you, draw near. Louder the music there!

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: O my dear father! Restoration hang

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Thy medicine on my lips; and let this kiss

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Repair those violent harms that my two sisters

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Have in thy reverence made!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Kind and dear princess!

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Had you not been their father, these white flakes

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Had challenged pity of them. Was this a face

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: To be opposed against the warring winds?

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: To stand against the deep dread-bolted thunder?

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: In the most terrible and nimble stroke

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Of quick, cross lightning? to watch—poor perdu!—

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: With this thin helm? Mine enemy’s dog,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Though he had bit me, should have stood that night

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Against my fire; and wast thou fain, poor father,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: To hovel thee with swine, and rogues forlorn,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: In short and musty straw? Alack, alack!

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: ’Tis wonder that thy life and wits at once

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Had not concluded all. He wakes; speak to him.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Madam, do you; ’tis fittest.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: How does my royal lord? How fares your majesty?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You do me wrong to take me out o’ the grave:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thou art a soul in bliss; but I am bound

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Upon a wheel of fire, that mine own tears

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Do scald like moulten lead.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Sir, do you know me?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You are a spirit, I know: when did you die?

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Still, still, far wide!

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: He’s scarce awake: let him alone awhile.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Where have I been? Where am I? Fair daylight?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I am mightily abused. I should e’en die with pity,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To see another thus. I know not what to say.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I will not swear these are my hands: let’s see;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I feel this pin prick. Would I were assured

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Of my condition!

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: O, look upon me, sir,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: And hold your hands in benediction o’er me:

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: No, sir, you must not kneel.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Pray, do not mock me:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I am a very foolish fond old man,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Fourscore and upward, not an hour more nor less;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And, to deal plainly,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I fear I am not in my perfect mind.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Methinks I should know you, and know this man;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Yet I am doubtful for I am mainly ignorant

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What place this is; and all the skill I have

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Remembers not these garments; nor I know not

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Where I did lodge last night. Do not laugh at me;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: For, as I am a man, I think this lady

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To be my child Cordelia.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: And so I am, I am.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Be your tears wet? yes, ’faith. I pray, weep not:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: If you have poison for me, I will drink it.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I know you do not love me; for your sisters

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Have, as I do remember, done me wrong:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You have some cause, they have not.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: No cause, no cause.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Am I in France?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: In your own kingdom, sir.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Do not abuse me.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Be comforted, good madam: the great rage,

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: You see, is kill’d in him: and yet it is danger

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: To make him even o’er the time he has lost.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Desire him to go in; trouble him no more

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Till further settling.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Will’t please your highness walk?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You must bear with me:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Pray you now, forget and forgive: I am old and foolish.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Holds it true, sir, that the Duke of Cornwall was so slain?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Most certain, sir.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Who is conductor of his people?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: As ’tis said, the bastard son of Gloucester.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: They say Edgar, his banished son, is with the Earl of Kent in Germany.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Report is changeable. ’Tis time to look about; the powers of the kingdom approach apace.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The arbitrement is like to be bloody. Fare you well, sir.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: My point and period will be throughly wrought,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Or well or ill, as this day’s battle’s fought.

stage_directions.text: Exeunt all but Kent and Gentleman.

glosses.definition: Cordelia's thanks to the disguised Kent
fit to be revealed
Lear's mental discord figured as musical disharmony
a father transformed by his children's cruelty
the storm — recalled
forlorn sentinel — soldier on a lost post

glosses.phrase: I know not what to say
glosses.definition: Lear, waking, cannot place himself
Lear's vision of his punishment — the pagan/Christian image of a soul's eternal torment
Lear cannot believe Cordelia is real
Cordelia's restraint — she lets Lear find her at his own pace

glosses.phrase: Pray you now, forget and forgive
glosses.definition: Lear's most wounded line of self-knowledge
fit; proper
father transformed by his children's cruelty
Doctor's address to Cordelia
cure
the bodily abuse Lear suffered
lost sentinel — a soldier on a hopeless post
foolish; doting
eighty or more years old — Lear's reported age

glosses.phrase: I know not what to say
glosses.definition: Lear finding no speech
mistreated
his only plea — the play's most disarming phrase
realm — Lear does not remember what he did with it

annotations.title: Lear wakes — 'You do me wrong to take me out o' the grave'
annotations.body: Lear waking to Cordelia is the play's most tender scene. He does not immediately recognise her; he thinks he is dead and bound on 'a wheel of fire.' 'Pray, do not mock me: / I am a very foolish fond old man.' The vocabulary is minimized — almost no Latinate words, almost all monosyllables. Shakespeare strips Lear's language in precise proportion to how far he is from his 1.1 grandeur. 'I think this lady / To be my child Cordelia.' 'And so I am, I am.' Reunion delivered through uncertainty — Lear finding identity through recognising hers first. The Folio cuts some lines from this scene that Q1 preserves (another textual divergence); modern editions restore them because they make Lear's humility more complete. This is the play's closest approach to redemption, and Shakespeare will destroy it in 5.3. But while it lasts, it is perfect.
sources: Stanley Cavell, 'The Avoidance of Love' in Disowning Knowledge (1987)

annotations.title: 'Pray, do not mock me' — the simplicity of restoration
annotations.body: Lear waking to Cordelia says 'Pray, do not mock me: / I am a very foolish fond old man, / Fourscore and upward, not an hour more nor less; / And, to deal plainly, / I fear I am not in my perfect mind.' The speech is built from monosyllables and plain structure — no subordinate clauses, no Latinate vocabulary, no rhetorical figures. Shakespeare is matching syntax to self. The Lear of 1.1 spoke in elaborate subordinations; the Lear of 4.7 speaks in hesitation and plain fact. The humility is structural. Cordelia says 'No cause, no cause' — three syllables answering 'Have I caused you to suffer?' — and the exchange settles the play's quietest minute. Only the play's catastrophe in 5.3 can undo this tenderness. This is Shakespeare at his most economical.

trials.prompt: What is distinctive about Lear's language when he wakes to Cordelia in 4.7?
options: Stripped to near-monosyllables ('a very foolish fond old man,' 'I am, I am') — Shakespeare minimises diction in proportion to Lear's distance from his 1.1 grandeur

trials.prompt: What does Shakespeare achieve in 4.7 by stripping Lear's speech to monosyllables and plain fact?
options: Syntax matches self — the elaborate Lear of 1.1 contracts to plain hesitation; the humility is structural, not only emotional
